era_context: The early 13th century was characterized by the rapid expansion of the Mongol Empire under Ögedei Khan, the fragmentation of Islamic dynasties in the Middle East and North Africa, the consolidation of European feudal kingdoms, and the final decades of the Crusader states. The Jin Dynasty in northern China was under severe pressure from Mongol forces, and the Southern Song Dynasty was preparing for the inevitable Mongol conquest.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Oceania and Pacific islands (no written records)
Americas (no written records in Old World sources)

events.title: Mongol siege of Kaifeng begins; Jin Dynasty in crisis
events.description: The Mongols, under Ögedei Khan and his generals Tolui and Subutai, began the siege of Kaifeng, the Jin Dynasty capital, in 1232. The Jin Dynasty, already weakened by decades of conflict with the Mongols, was now facing its final crisis. The siege would last until early 1234, resulting in the complete destruction of the Jin state. The siege of Kaifeng was one of the most destructive sieges of the medieval world, with enormous casualties among the civilian population.

sources.name: Yuanshi (Official History of the Yuan Dynasty)
Jin Dynasty records and Chinese chronicles

events.certainty_note: The beginning of the Kaifeng siege in 1232 is well-attested in multiple sources.

events.title: Al-Ma'mun becomes Almohad Caliph; dynasty in decline
events.description: Al-Ma'mun became the Almohad Caliph in 1232, succeeding Al-Mustansir II. However, his reign marked a period of continued decline for the Almohad Dynasty. The dynasty was losing territory in Iberia to Christian kingdoms and facing challenges from rising dynasties in North Africa, particularly the Hafsids in Tunisia and the Marinids in Morocco. Al-Ma'mun's reign (1232–1242) would be characterized by internal strife and territorial losses.
events.certainty_note: Al-Ma'mun's accession in 1232 is documented in Islamic chronicles.

events.title: Iltutmish consolidates power in the Delhi Sultanate
events.description: Shams al-Din Iltutmish, the founder of the Slave Dynasty (Mamluk Dynasty) in Delhi, was at the height of his power in 1232. He had consolidated Turkish rule in northern India and was expanding the sultanate's territory. Iltutmish was establishing the administrative and military structures that would define the Delhi Sultanate for centuries. His reign (1211–1236) was a period of expansion and consolidation.
key_figures: Shams al-Din Iltutmish (Sultan of Delhi, r. 1211–1236)
events.certainty_note: Iltutmish's consolidation of power is documented in Islamic chronicles, though specific events in 1232 are not always clearly dated.

events.title: Mongol campaigns in Persia and the Khwarazmian remnants
events.description: Mongol forces continued to consolidate control over Persia and Central Asia in 1232, eliminating remaining pockets of resistance from the destroyed Khwarazmian Empire. These campaigns were part of Ögedei Khan's broader strategy of expanding Mongol dominion. The consolidation of Mongol control over Persia and Central Asia facilitated trade and communication across the Silk Road.
key_figures: Chagatai Khan (Mongol ruler of Central Asia)

sources.name: Jami' al-tawarikh (Compendium of Chronicles) by Rashid al-Din

events.certainty_note: Mongol campaigns in Persia are documented in Islamic and Mongol sources, though specific events in 1232 are less clearly dated.

events.title: Henry III becomes King of England; beginning of his long reign
events.description: Henry III became King of England in 1216 (following his father King John's death), but 1232 marks a significant point in his reign when he began to assert more independent authority after the regency period. Henry III's reign (1216–1272) was one of the longest in English history and was marked by conflicts with the barons, the development of Parliament, and struggles over royal prerogatives. By 1232, Henry was consolidating his power and beginning to pursue his own policies.

sources.name: Chronicles of Matthew Paris, contemporary chronicler
Royal records and charters, contemporary documentation

events.certainty_note: Henry III's reign is well-documented in English chronicles and royal records.

disconfirming_evidence: The exact date of the beginning of the Kaifeng siege is sometimes given as 1231 or 1232, with some sources placing it in late 1231 and others in early 1232. The significance of Al-Ma'mun's accession in 1232 is debated—some scholars argue the Almohad decline was already well underway before his reign. Henry III's assertion of independent authority in 1232 is not universally agreed upon; some sources suggest this process was more gradual.
historiographic_note: The year 1232 is moderately well-documented for East Asia (Mongol siege of Kaifeng), the Islamic world (Almohad succession, Mongol campaigns), and Europe (Henry III's reign). The historical record is weighted toward political and military events. Economic and social developments are underrepresented. Chinese, Islamic, and European sources dominate; African, Southeast Asian, and American sources are absent.

graph_edges.note: The beginning of the Kaifeng siege in 1232 led to its intensification in 1233 and the fall of the Jin Dynasty in 1234.
Al-Ma'mun's accession in 1232 was part of the broader Almohad decline that continued in 1234.
Iltutmish's consolidation of power in 1232 was followed by his death and a succession crisis in 1234.
